sentences.text: Yes.
No.
No, I don't-- I, I don't recall us sitting together.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It could have been we're not.
You know, I just-- it's not something that remained in my memory.
No.
No, none of my teachers were Jewish.
As a matter of fact, some of them were real, real Nazis.
But we had some teachers which, which were a little more understanding, you know, from  the old school.
The younger teachers were really rabid Nazis, you know.
And--
Well, you know, the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if you-- if you raised your hand, or something like this, they didn't-- they didn't give you-- didn't call on you.
Or, you know-- it just would make you feel uncomfortable, you know.
You knew you that were different.
They would make you feel you're different.

On the other hand, you had some other teachers, who-- some older teachers, OK, who were a little more understanding and, you know, tried to do their best without, without showing that they are-- that they're not Nazis.
You know, everybody had to belong to the Nazi party, in those days.
So my recollection of the-- of my school years, the four years of elementary school, was, was not too bad, you know, not too bad.
It's-- sometimes you got some fights.
And the kids, you know, kids used to beat you up.
But it's-- it was more or less kids' stuff, you know.
There was always some guy-- there was always a bully in every class.
But, generally speaking, I didn't get hurt or anything like that.
But I tried to keep-- we tried to keep away.
Because after-- usually after school, I went to cheder, you know.
So I had-- I didn't have much time to really-- to horse around, so to speak.
I only stayed there for, for, for four years-- elementary school, you see?
In Germany, that-- if I recall, the school system was, you, you, you, you had to go for eight years to school.
That was obligatory.
You, you spent four years in Volksschule-- you know, elementary school.
And then, if you could afford it, and if you passed the exam, you could matriculate through to the gymnasium, which was was a combination of college and high school.
If not, you continued on to so-called high school for another four years.
Then, if you-- then you could drop out.
As long as you competed your eight years of schooling, you were all right.
Naturally, every, every kid-- every Jewish kid, matriculated to the gymnasium, which was a Jewish school, called the Carlebach Gymnasium.
And as I mentioned, my doctor, I think, Dr.
Borg was a teacher at that gymnasium.
And there you-- there I got my first, first lessons in English.
We had-- English was one of the scores.
And then you continued on, you know, for another eight years, which, naturally, none of us-- none of us did, at least, not me.
Because in 1938, the school was burned down on Kristallnacht.
Yes, I, I remember it.
You know, those days, you didn't have television or radio, you know.
And I went to school-- I went to school.
See, nothing happened in our house, OK?
Nothing happened to our house.
We, we were-- we were in an apartment building.
And I guess there was other people living there, too.
They were not-- we were not all Jewish in our house-- our apartment complex.
We're not-- we were not all Jews.
So I guess they didn't-- they bothered more with businesses and the synagogues.
So I went to school.
And all of a sudden, I hear that the, the, the-- there's no school.
The Carlebach was, was, was burned or-- and vandalized.
And then, before you know it, word got around that the stores and Jewish businesses were vandalized and burnt, and the synagogues burnt, vandalized, and something like this.
I just went home.
And this-- we stayed home.
Did you understand why it was happening?
I understood that a Jewish fellow-- I think his name was Grynzspan, something like that.
And, you know, this is history.
You can look it up.
He murdered-- huh?
No, no, I could read the newspapers, you know.
I, I, I was an avid reader of the-- to this day, I, I don't function-- [LAUGHS] I don't function right unless I have a newspaper.

I must have picked up the newspaper or seen the newspaper where this Jewish fellow-- I think his name was-- I believe his name was Grynszpan, something like that-- murdered a, a, a second secretary of the German Embassy in Paris, I believe.
The name was-- his name was vom Rath, or something like that.
In retaliation-- you know, in retaliation, you know, it was a good excuse for, for the Nazis to, to just go and destroy anything that was Jewish.
I saw glass.
And I saw-- I saw-- on certain businesses, I saw glass.
See, you walked to school, you know.
There was no-- there was no school buses, or something like that.
And I saw, in certain places-- were glass, and everything else.
And I just went right home.
And we stayed home, you know.
And my father came home, too, right away.
He didn't know, either.
He went-- he went to his business.
But nothing happened to his business because he didn't-- he didn't have a store.
He was a wholesaler, up on-- up on the second floor, somewhere, in the-- in the business district.
But anybody that had stores was just-- glass was smashed, and everything else.
So he came right home.
And we just-- and we just-- we said, stay put.
We stayed in the house.
That's what I remember about Kristallnacht.

Oh, yeah, plenty of-- first of all, you couldn't-- you couldn't go-- a lot, a lot of stores, the department stores, for example, had the sign 'Jews'-- 'Jews not allowed' or 'not desired.' You know, you couldn't go-- you couldn't go to, like, a Broadway or, or a Robinson, or whatever, just Macey's, you know.
The only thing you could go in was some-- there were some Jewish-- there was some Jewish stores.
And, you know, you could go in there.
A lot of stores, even smallest stores, had signs that-- 'Jews not wanted.' You could not go to the theater.
You could not go to the movies.
Later on, you couldn't even go to the public park.
They used to have a little section left for them.
It was called the 'Judenpark,' you know, the Jew Park.
So I used to go there.
A lot of-- a lot of signs of anti-Semitism that you-- I experienced that, sure.
Yes, yes, they explained to us what was going on, and they--
Well, you know, it's, it's-- there's people in power now.
They're, you know, in the government, which is anti-Jewish.
And they're persecuting Jews.
But, you know, never-- nobody ever expected things to happen what happened.
We-- there was a time we tried to-- we tried to-- my father, I understand, tried to, to get exit visas, to leave.
But really, we had no-- we had nobody in America to get an-- you needed an affidavit of support to go to the United States.
We needed a permit to get to England.
And, otherwise, we were not that wealthy for us to buy-- I think you could buy yourself a ticket to, to, to Shanghai.
That was another place that we were-- people could go.
There was really no place to go.
I remember, at the end, when it was too late, I think my father was able to get a visa for the-- for Santo Domingo, the Dominican Republic.
But by that time, it was, was too late to go.
You see, nobody wanted Jews.
And I experienced that after the war, and-- which we'll come to that, how I really felt again that nobody wanted Jews-- nobody wants Jews.
So I felt anti-Semitism in Germany.
Yeah.
Things, things changed, you know.
It was-- they imposed a lot of restrictions.
Well, like I mentioned before, you know, you couldn't do this, and you couldn't-- you couldn't go to the stores.
And you couldn't-- certainly, you couldn't go to the public-- you couldn't go to the public libraries or the public swimming pools.
[SIGHS]
Yeah.
No, I don't remember the headlines.
But I remember-- there was two, two papers that were prominently displayed.
You see, I didn't have any money to go and buy the paper, you know, even though, in those days, they were a nickel apiece.
But you had these-- you had these stands, you know, where-- and that's where the paper were open.
And there was two, two prominent papers, which you really didn't  have to buy.
You could read them.
One was the-- called the which was the Nazi-- the, the-- was the, the organ of the Nazi party.
And the other one was was the-- it was nothing else to-- it was a pornographic, anti-Semitic [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] journal.
As a matter of fact, the guy-- the guy who was the editor on this, a fellow by the name of Julius Streicher, he was hanged in Nuremberg.
I wouldn't-- S-- S-T-R-E-I-C-H-E-R.
He was-- from what I-- when I read, I started to read-- when I read William Shirer, he was a real obnoxious character to begin with.
So he was doing nothing else but Jew-baiting.
And so these papers were prominently displayed.
So, you know, if anything was going on-- his paper was, was half-truth.
It's like-- how should I say this-- like, like the to a certain degree, but really, nothing else but anti-Semitic things.
Showing a-- showing what Jews do-- that they poison wells and rape German women, [LAUGHS] and stuff like that.
You know, really, in those days, I really didn't understand quite what that meant.
And-- but the was the-- that organ of the Nazi party.
And anything goes on, you just had to read it-- look at it and read it.
And it'd tell you what's going on.
And then, then you had the radio, you know.
Everybody had a radio.
You had, had a radio in your house.
TV was not invented yet.
You had to watch your step.
And you had to know who you're talking to, and what you're saying.
You, you had to be-- you had to be alert and be on the ball.
Yes, I remember.
They were talking about we should try-- and should get out.
But, you know, my father had a business.
We had a nice-- we had a nice apartment.
And, you know, he didn't want to-- nobody, you know, figured-- we  always figured things are going to get better, you know.
How long-- how long can this last?
And then again, I'm sure they tried to-- they talked about getting out.
But there was no-- we didn't-- we didn't have the means to buy, like some other people, to buy their way out.
And we were-- we were not wealthy.
We were comfortable, but not wealthy.
So I'm sure that, you know-- I'm sure that at times they tried to-- they thought about getting out.

What happened then was the first-- the first big incarnation would have happened was, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] sometimes in-- after Kristallnacht, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which was November of 1938-- I think in the beginning of 1939, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] our whole family was arrested.
Why?
Because we were Polish citizens, OK?
They-- see, my father-- his father became Poland.
So he became-- he became a Polish citizen, OK?
And they took us-- they took the whole family to the railroad station in Leipzig.
They separated the men from the women and children.
How old was I, at that point?
Let's see. 26-- I was about 12.
My father must have been about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 45.
My mother was 40.
I'm just-- I'm trying to figure quickly.
But, well, they're young, you know, with young children.
And they were-- took us all to the railroad station.
They separated the men from the women.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What they did, they deported-- they wanted to deport all the Jewish-- all the Polish-- this was-- the war hasn't started yet, OK?
They were-- they shipped them-- they shipped them to Poland.
Now, my father was taken to the Polish border.
And then, all of a sudden, they, they, they deported him, said, get out!
And he was sent to Poland.
Mother, myself, and my brother, we remained in a-- in a waiting hall, there, at the Leipzig Station.
We were supposed to be shipped out later on, OK?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a moment there when nobody passed.
And my mother said, hey, let's see if we can get out.
So my brother and I, we just walked out.
And I guess nobody was looking.
We were kids.
And we just walked out of there.
And I think we went to-- went to some-- went home to some Jewish neighbor.
My mother remained there.
She fainted.
She, she, she faked that she fainted.
Now my uncle-- her brother, her half-brother-- he was a Romanian citizen.
Because, [LAUGH] you know, Tschernowitz became Romania.
Somehow, he had a Romanian passport.
And he went there.
And he said that's his sister.
She's a Romanian citizen.
In those days, you were Romanian citizen, oh, they, they let you go.
So before-- now, that we were-- we were back home, in the apartment.
But my father was deported to Poland.
But my father came-- he came back.
He came back in the beginning of-- in the middle of 1939.
He applied for a visa.
He said he had to-- he had to, you know, close out his business.
So he, he had the chance to come back to Leipzig.
So you were all together again?
We were all together again, you know, for a short while.
And September 1, 1939, about 5 o'clock in the morning, we had a knock at the door by, by the Gestapo.
We want Solomon Weininger.
Quick!
You know?
No.
What is it?
Just don't ask questions.
Just come.
He just grabbed-- put his pants on, and his-- he had his medals there.
They took his medals and his shirt and pants.
And that's it.
They took him.
It's the last time I've seen my father.
He was taken to a labor camp in Leipzig, I think, for about a week.
And then he was taken to the concentration camp Sachsenhausen, in Oranienburg, near Berlin.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he stayed there.
I think he sent-- he was able to send one card.
In those days, you know, the Germans were still organized.
They let you-- they let you send a postcard.
But you ?] [? have to-- ?] this was in Germany, you know?
So, you know, Dear-- I remember-- Dear folks, I'm doing fine.
Don't worry about it.
Hope to see you soon.
That's all.
In April-- and, in the meantime, we used to get notices I mean, other  kids-- other people-- in the shul, you know, on the street, that their fathers died eventually.
We-- you know, everybody was waiting for the telegram.
And the telegram came-- came to us.
And it said, like anybody else, 'Husband died of pneumonia.
Camp Kommando.' But everybody died of Kommando.
It was a form letter.
You know, nobody died of cold, or, or-- everybody died of pneumonia.
So that's--
no, no.
I have no-- just, you know, Gestapo.
They all had-- they used to wear their-- they used to wear their leather, leather jackets, you know.
And they used to have, like, a-- it's like, today, you have-- like a-- like a coin, you know, showing they're Gestapo.
You know, you knew it was Gestapo.
And it's just-- no idea what they looked like.
Well, I-- you know, I was shocked, you know, that they took him.
But I, I just didn't know.
I was too young, really, to, to comprehend the seriousness.
You know, I thought maybe they take him-- they'd take him to the police and talk to him and then let him go again, something like that, you know.
I didn't know that he was going to be sent to the concentration camp.
But--
Well, she, she asked them, what, what do you want?
You know, they-- shut up!
You know, we-- they didn't-- they didn't-- we couldn't talk.
She just told him, take a sweater with you.
Or-- you know how it is, how a wife is, and everything else-- shoes and socks.
That's, that's all he could take with him, you know.
I think he had that-- 10 minutes to get dressed.
Don't forget, it was 5 o'clock in the morning, you know.
He was sleeping-- his pajamas.
So he just-- he just barely put his pants on.
And actually, my mother was crying, you know.
She probably knew-- had a better idea than us.
And she probably didn't want us children to really know what happened, you know.
She tried to, you know, protect us from that as much as she could.
My, my father-- OK.
Let me-- just let me tell you quickly what happened.
A few months later, we got a little box, like everybody else, which contained the ashes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now what's in that box, I have no idea.
It could have been just plain dirt, or-- I'm sure they did not cremate his body and then preserve his ashes into this, this box.
It was just a formality.
They sent us a box back, which supposedly was his ashes.
And we buried this box [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in proper form, at the old Jewish cemetery in Leipzig.
I went to see it.
I went to find-- that's one of the reasons-- against my better judgment, but at the urging of my wife.
It is-- we went-- we were on a trip to Israel.
And my wife said, do yourself a favor.
Go back to Germany once more and find-- and see if you can find your father's grave, say Kaddish.
And that's-- at least get this-- get this over, which I did.
I was able to find the grave.
It was neglected.
But it was still there.
So, in 1988, I went back to Leipzig for the first time for three days.
And I-- so that's, that's, that's the end of Solomon Weininger.
I did-- I did one other thing.
I had an aunt who lived in Israel, who died-- my father's sister.
I met her.
I lived-- I was with her, and everything else.
She, she died in Israel.
And at her tombstone, I made-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we made another tombstone on top of it, OK?
Symbolically, it's showing my father, mother, and brother.
So that, symbolically, they are buried in Israel.
Because, see, now, my mother and brother-- I have no-- I have no idea.
But at least, there is a-- there's a memorial.
Their names are still-- they're on a tombstone, in Haifa.
What is your father's sister's name?
Her name was Klara.
Klara-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] oh, God, I should know the name, but I forgot it right now.
Her name was Klara.
And she lived-- she found me.
She found me in the-- in, in the United States.
And I've seen her many times.
She died, oh, I would say, about maybe six, seven years ago.
She's buried in Haifa.
Yeah.
September 1st, and it's 6 o'clock in the morning.
Yeah.
Well, we just-- nothing.
We just, you know, we, we stayed there.
And we, we wanted to know what, what happened-- what happened to his business.
We found out that they-- at the same time, they just-- somebody went in his business and just took it over.
OK?
And we were-- we had a little-- my wife-- my mother had a little money hidden.
We were just living basically on welfare, you know, on what you had.
And just life was not the same anymore.
You know, we were without a father.
We went to-- like many other kids, you know, half the shul went up and said Kaddish.
And you just-- you just continued living, you know, on-- was ration cards.
You-- we only-- Jews, Jews only got half.
The war-- that's the-- see, the war started.
Jews only got half the ration cards that the general population got.
And you can only buy it in, in, in-- at so-called Jew, Jew stores.
And there was real, real discrimination, you know, against anything.
So then, my-- I think word got around that the Aliyah Bet, the youth aliyah, was going to try and get some-- save some of the kids.
But they start taking other people to the concentration camps, you know.
Before you know it, they start taking kids.
But in those days, I think, they, they stopped at age 15.
Anything below age 15, they-- in those days, they didn't take them.
But other people, they just took them and, you know-- And so the youth aliyah-- the-- so my mother inquired.
And they said, we'll take one of your boys.
We were trying to reach-- trying to reach Palestine.
So I was one-- I was one of the boys.
My wife-- my thought, you know, at least get me out.
And then, I said, you know, I'll try and get you out afterwards.
And so my mother signed me up for this-- for this transport to go to Vienna.
I think in the Jewish community, somehow, we had some money.
But the youth aliyah-- Rechaa Freier was one of the sponsors of this thing.
Yeah, Recha-- R-E-C-H-A-A-A, F-R-E-I-E-R-- Freier.
And people in Hadassah-- I think she was one of the disciples of Henrietta Szold of Hadassah.
Anybody with youth aliyah, this is a revered name, Rechaa Freier.
And, before I know, I was-- I remember the night my mother took me to the railroad station.
It was, was dark.
It was dark, you know.
And I cried.
I, I, I, I-- like I knew it's the last time I'm going to see her.
And I was only-- I was only, what, 13, 14 years old.
I was never bar mitzvahed in-- I was never bar mitzvahed, by the way.
I'll come to that [LAUGHS]-- I'll come to that later on.
And, and we took-- we went to-- we went to the train.
And she turned me over to one of the leaders, which I don't know who they are.
I have no recollection whatsoever.
I just know I bitterly cried, all the way to the railroad station.
And we took the train.
And then I went to the transport to Vienna.
My mother and my brother stayed-- remained home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think in 1943, I heard-- I didn't hear from them anymore.
I-- as a matter of fact, I, I heard, after the war, that they were taken to the ghetto in Riga, Riga, Latvia.
And that, that-- because I met somebody in Israel who knew my brother.
He went to school with my brother.
And he said he was there.
But he was transferred to, to Buchenwald.
And he saw my mother and brother.
And he said they, they were there.
And then the whole ghetto, before the Nazis retreated, was, was, was eliminated.
They exterminated everybody.
I inquired there, went through the Red Cross and the Vatican, and everything else.
And it was confirmed that-- but I have no proof.
I have no death certificate, no-- nobody knows where they're buried, what happened to them.
